topic: Standard Separation Techniques for Mixtures (Part III)

subUnits.title: 7.1 Chromatography and Sublimation
notes: Chromatography:
• A technique used to separate mixtures of soluble substances
• Based on differences in solubility and adsorption of components
• Types of Chromatography:
1. Paper Chromatography (most common in schools)
2. Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC)
3. Column Chromatography
4. Gas Chromatography
Paper Chromatography Process:
1. Draw a pencil line near the bottom of chromatography paper
2. Place a small spot of mixture on the pencil line
3. Place paper in a container with solvent (water, ethanol, etc.)
4. Solvent moves up the paper by capillary action
5. Different components travel at different rates
6. Components separate into distinct spots or bands
Principle:
• Components have different solubilities in the solvent
• Components have different affinities for the paper (stationary phase)
• More soluble components travel further
• Less soluble components travel shorter distances
Rf Value (Retention Factor):
Rf = Distance traveled by substance / Distance traveled by solvent
• Rf value is characteristic for each substance
• Used to identify substances by comparing with known standards
Applications:
• Separating ink dyes (different colors in black ink)
• Separating plant pigments (chlorophyll, carotenes)
• Testing purity of substances
• Identifying substances in forensic science
• Analyzing amino acids and drugs
Sublimation:
• Direct change of state from solid to gas without passing through liquid state
• Used to separate substances that sublime from those that don't
Substances that Sublime:
• Iodine (I₂) - purple vapor
• Ammonium chloride (NH₄Cl) - white vapor
• Naphthalene (mothballs)
• Dry ice (solid CO₂)
• Camphor
Process:
1. Heat the mixture gently
2. Sublimable substance changes directly to vapor
3. Vapor rises and condenses on cold surface
4. Pure solid (sublimate) collects on cold surface
5. Non-sublimable substance remains in dish
Example: Separating ammonium chloride from sand
• Heat mixture of NH₄Cl and sand
• NH₄Cl sublimes (solid → gas)
• NH₄Cl vapor condenses on cold watch glass
• Sand remains in evaporating dish

quiz.q: Chromatography separates substances based on:
quiz.explanation: Chromatography separates components based on their different solubilities in the solvent and different affinities for the stationary phase.

quiz.q: In paper chromatography, the solvent moves up the paper by:
quiz.explanation: The solvent moves up the chromatography paper by capillary action, carrying the mixture components with it.

quiz.q: The Rf value is calculated as:
opts: Distance of solvent / Distance of substance
Distance of substance / Distance of solvent
Distance of substance × Distance of solvent
Distance of solvent - Distance of substance
quiz.explanation: Rf = Distance traveled by substance ÷ Distance traveled by solvent. Rf values help identify substances.

quiz.q: Which component travels furthest in chromatography?
quiz.explanation: The most soluble component in the solvent travels the furthest up the paper, while less soluble components travel shorter distances.

quiz.q: Chromatography can be used to:
opts: Separate mixtures of soluble substances like ink dyes
quiz.explanation: Chromatography is used to separate mixtures of soluble substances, such as separating different colored dyes in ink.

quiz.q: Sublimation is the process where:
quiz.explanation: Sublimation is the direct change from solid to gas without passing through the liquid state.

quiz.q: Which substance sublimes?
quiz.explanation: Ammonium chloride (NH₄Cl) sublimes when heated, changing directly from solid to white vapor.

quiz.q: To separate ammonium chloride from sand, use:
quiz.explanation: Sublimation is used because NH₄Cl sublimes when heated while sand does not, allowing easy separation.

quiz.q: In sublimation, the pure solid collected is called:
quiz.explanation: The pure solid that forms when vapor condenses during sublimation is called the sublimate.

quiz.q: An application of chromatography is:
opts: Testing purity of substances and identifying drugs
quiz.explanation: Chromatography is used in forensic science, drug testing, food analysis, and checking purity of substances.

subUnits.title: 7.2 Pure and Impure Substances
notes: Pure Substances:
• Have fixed and constant composition
• Have definite physical properties (melting point, boiling point, density)
• Cannot be separated into simpler substances by physical methods
• Examples: Elements (gold, oxygen, iron) and Compounds (water, salt, sugar)
Characteristics of Pure Substances:
1. Sharp melting point - melts at a specific temperature
2. Sharp boiling point - boils at a specific temperature
3. Constant composition throughout
4. Cannot be separated by physical methods
Impure Substances (Mixtures):
• Have variable composition
• Physical properties vary with composition
• Can be separated by physical methods
• Examples: Air, seawater, soil, alloys
Characteristics of Impure Substances:
1. Melting occurs over a range of temperatures
2. Boiling occurs over a range of temperatures
3. Composition can vary
4. Can be separated by physical methods
Testing Purity:
1. Melting Point Test:
• Pure substances melt at sharp, definite temperature
• Impure substances melt over a temperature range
• Example: Pure ice melts at 0°C exactly
• Impure ice melts below 0°C over a range
2. Boiling Point Test:
• Pure substances boil at sharp, definite temperature
• Impure substances boil over a temperature range
• Example: Pure water boils at 100°C at standard pressure
• Salt water boils above 100°C
3. Chromatography Test:
• Pure substance gives single spot on chromatogram
• Impure substance gives multiple spots
Effects of Impurities:
• Lower the melting point (melting point depression)
• Raise the boiling point (boiling point elevation)
• Cause melting/boiling to occur over a range
Examples:
• Adding salt to ice lowers melting point (used in ice cream making)
• Adding antifreeze to water lowers freezing point
• Salt water boils at higher temperature than pure water

quiz.q: A pure substance has:
quiz.explanation: Pure substances have fixed, constant composition and definite physical properties like sharp melting and boiling points.

quiz.q: An impure substance melts:
quiz.explanation: Impure substances melt over a range of temperatures, while pure substances melt at a sharp, definite temperature.

quiz.q: Pure water boils at:
quiz.explanation: Pure water boils at exactly 100°C at standard atmospheric pressure (1 atm or 760 mmHg).

quiz.q: Adding salt to water causes the boiling point to:
quiz.explanation: Impurities raise the boiling point. Salt water boils above 100°C (boiling point elevation).

quiz.q: Which test can determine purity of a substance?
opts: Melting point and boiling point tests
quiz.explanation: Melting point and boiling point tests are reliable methods to determine purity. Pure substances have sharp, definite melting and boiling points.

quiz.q: A pure substance on chromatography gives:
quiz.explanation: A pure substance gives a single spot on a chromatogram, while an impure substance gives multiple spots.

quiz.q: Adding impurities to a pure substance causes melting point to:
quiz.explanation: Impurities lower the melting point of a pure substance (melting point depression).

quiz.q: Which is a pure substance?
quiz.explanation: Distilled water is a pure substance (compound). Air, seawater, and soil are mixtures (impure substances).

quiz.q: Salt water boils at a temperature:
quiz.explanation: Salt water (impure) boils above 100°C due to boiling point elevation caused by dissolved salt.

quiz.q: An example of an impure substance is:
quiz.explanation: Air is a mixture of gases (nitrogen, oxygen, carbon dioxide, etc.), making it an impure substance.
